data.title: Sliding Oil Prices Have Reopened the Door to Russian Crude — Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

  • Brent crude oil is down 0.6% to $62.94 a barrel
  • European benchmark gas is down 2.3% to EUR34.60 a megawatt-hour
  • Gold futures are down 0.45% to $3,329.0 a troy ounce
  • LME three-month copper futures are down 0.1% at $9,557.50 a metric ton

TOP STORY:

Sliding Oil Prices Have Reopened the Door to Russian Crude

Earlier this year, the Western energy industry began to calculate how it might tiptoe back into Russia if there is peace in Ukraine.

One group isn't hanging around. A cadre of Greek shipping magnates has sent a stream of ships to Russian ports in recent months, stepping back into the fold as the go-to distributors of Moscow's crude to the world.

The jump back into the lucrative trade follows a fall in oil prices this year. Handling Russian oil is legal under Western sanctions if the price is below $60 a barrel.

The Greek moves also reflect signs of a rapprochement between the U.S. and Moscow soon after President Trump's return to office. But Trump's recent criticism of President Vladimir Putin-including a warning of further sanctions-shows why many other Western companies have been cautious about rebuilding their businesses in Russia.

OTHER STORIES:

Soaring Costs Expose a Trans-Atlantic Chocolate Divide

Almost everyone loves chocolate, whether it's a Hershey's Kiss, a Toblerone or a dark Lindt bar.

But a historic surge in cocoa prices is testing just how deep that love really runs. And it turns out Europeans may be a bit more committed to chocolate than their American counterparts. That gap in loyalty has real implications for global confectionary companies.

This isn't to dismiss America's sweet tooth. The U.S. accounts for about a quarter of global chocolate sales, and many consumers have a deeply emotional connection to it-often tied to childhood memories of biting into that Snickers bar, scooping up M&M's or unwrapping that familiar brown-and-silver Hershey's foil.

--

Nippon Steel to Invest $6 Billion for Electric Arc Furnaces to Cut Carbon Emissions

Nippon Steel plans to invest $6 billion to increase production of steel using electric arc furnaces in a bid to reduce carbon emissions.

The Japanese steelmaker said Friday that it would invest 868.7 billion yen, equivalent to $6.02 billion, to establish three electric furnaces in Japan and that it expects the Japanese government to provide up to Y251.4 billion in support.

Nippon Steel said the investment will increase production capacity by about 2.9 million tons a year, with operations scheduled to commence by the fiscal year starting April 2029.

--

BP Appoints Former Devon Energy Chief David Hager as Nonexecutive Director

BP has appointed the former chief executive of U.S energy company Devon Energy, David Hager, as a nonexecutive director at a time when it is seeking to more than double its production of oil and gas in the U.S. by the end of the decade.

The British energy company said Friday that Hager brings a deep knowledge of the U.S. upstream oil-and-gas industry. His appointment will be effective from Monday.

--

Lynas Inks Tie-Up With State Government in Malaysia to Target Local Heavy Rare-Earths Supply

Lynas Rare Earths has struck an agreement with the strategic investment arm of Malaysia's Kelantan state government, aiming to develop ionic clay deposits that are rich in heavy rare earths needed for electric vehicles and electronic gadgets.

The miner said Friday that it has signed a memorandum of understanding with Kelantan's Menteri Besar Inc., or MB Inc., to "strengthen, promote and develop cooperation for the growth of the Malaysian rare-earths industry" within the state. The nonbinding agreement also provides a framework for the pair to negotiate a deal for the future supply of mixed rare-earths carbonate feedstock to Lynas, the company said.

Australia-based Lynas, which runs a processing plant near the Malaysian city of Kuantan, today sources all of its rare earths from the Mt. Weld mine in Western Australia. It has previously said its Malaysia plant--and a processing facility it plans to build in the U.S.--have been designed to take so-called third-party feedstock as it becomes available.

MARKET TALKS:

Oil Prices Rise on Inventories Data But Gains Subdued Ahead of OPEC+ Meeting — Market Talk

1147 GMT - Oil prices rise, with Brent crude up 1% at $63.98 a barrel and WTI up 1.1% at $61.60 a barrel. U.S. oil inventory data showed a larger-than-expected draw of nearly 3 million barrels, which should support higher prices, Trade Nation's David Morrison says in a note. However, Brent remains on track for a small weekly decline overall and traders appear unwilling to expose themselves to rallies or selloffs, Morrison writes. This is understandable given analysts expect OPEC+ to announce another production increase for July this weekend, as they unwind existing output cuts, Morrison says. So far, each increase has been bigger than consensus expectations, which has weighed on prices so far this year, Morrison adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Base Metal Prices Slide on Stronger U.S. Dollar, Macroeconomic Uncertainty — Market Talk

1108 GMT - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.1% at $9,558.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.2% at $2,444.50 a ton. A stronger U.S. dollar is keeping downward pressure on metal prices, making it more expensive for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. Tariff uncertainty is also hitting metals demand, as a federal appeals court allowed U.S. President Trump's levies to stay in effect while the administration appeals a court decision that found them illegal. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has also said that trade talks with China are "a bit stalled" and may need direct contact between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, raising further concerns for metal demand. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

Gold Futures Slide But Hold Higher Than Thursday's Sell-Off — Market Talk

1107 GMT - Gold futures fall, but hold above the lows seen in the prior session. Futures are down 0.75% at $3,318.80 a troy ounce. The precious metal fell as low as $3,269.90/oz in volatile trading on Thursday after a U.S. court declared most tariffs imposed by President Trump were illegal, but recovered quickly on uncertainty around whether levies would be suspended immediately. An appeals court ruled that the ruling will be paused for now and tariffs would remain in effect. The trade conflict is far from over as the ruling only concerns "reciprocal" tariffs rather than sector ones, which means the economic outlook remains gloomy and interest rate cut expectations will likely remain strong, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. This should benefit gold overall given the associated safe-haven demand, Commerzbank says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

Palm Oil Falls on Possible Profit-Taking — Market Talk

1021 GMT - Palm oil fell during the Asian session. Prices were likely pressured by the weaker overnight performance in rival oils and possible profit-taking after the recent sessions of gains, analysts at Kenanga Futures write in a note. A stronger ringgit also likely made exports less attractive, they add. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for August delivery fell MYR54 to MYR3,878 a ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

Oil Gains on Gasoline Demand Despite OPEC+ Concerns — Market Talk

0757 GMT - Crude oil ekes out some gains on rising gasoline demand hopes, despite concerns of increased OPEC+ supply. Brent crude and WTI are both up 0.3% at $63.52 and $61.14 a barrel, respectively. A huge spike in gasoline demand and dip in stocks ahead of the U.S. driving season has buoyed oil, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. That said, OPEC+ is expected to announce production hikes for July over the weekend. The exact size of the cartel's hike will be declared at the meeting but is expected to be larger than currently scheduled, ANZ writes. Sentiment toward oil hasn't been helped by a broader selloff across markets after data showed the U.S. economy shrank at the start of the year, ANZ adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

Gold Futures Tick Lower on Strengthening U.S. Dollar — Market Talk

0747 GMT - Gold futures slide on a stronger U.S. dollar. Futures are down 0.8% at $3,317.0 a troy ounce. The precious metal is on track for an overall weekly loss, though it remains up more than 22% in the year to date. Gold appears pressured by a stronger dollar, an alternative safe-haven asset in the face of market volatility. Market attention now turns to the release of Personal Consumption Expenditure data due later Friday. The inflation report--the preferred metric of the Federal Reserve--is expected to show prices moderated last month, opening the door for the Fed to ease monetary policy. Lower interest rates typically boost non-interest bearing bullion's appeal. Economists surveyed by FactSet expect consumer prices to rise 0.15% in April, lowering the annual rate to 2.2% from 2.3% in March. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

Iron Ore Falls on Declining Demand — Market Talk

0244 GMT - Iron ore falls in early Asian trade. Steel demand is in its off-season period, with domestic demand falling, given weather conditions, while the rush for exports are weakening, with the number of shipments from China to the U.S. falling marginally, analysts at Nanhua Futures say, noting the import demand from the U.S. is lower than expected. Fundamentals for iron ore remain good but the market is pessimistic about the future demand, they add. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is down 0.5% at CNY701.50/ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

data.title: Brazil Budget Deficit Narrows in April
data.description:
Brazil's nominal budget deficit narrowed to BRL 55.5 billion in April 2025, from BRL 69.6 billion in the corresponding month of the previous year and better than market expectations of a BRL 58.1 billion shortfall.

It also marked the best nominal result in three months.

Over the 12-month period, the nominal deficit totaled BRL 934.4 billion (7.76% of GDP), down from BRL 948.5 billion (7.92%) in March.

The public sector posted a primary surplus of BRL 14.1 billion in April, up from BRL 6.7 billion a year earlier.

The central government posted a BRL 16.2 billion surplus, while regional governments and state-owned firms recorded deficits of BRL 659 million and BRL 1.4 billion, respectively.

Gross public debt rose to 76.2% of GDP (BRL 9.2 trillion), a 0.3 percentage point increase from the previous month.

data.title: Brokerages dial down US recession forecasts amid tariff optimism
data.description:

Goldman Sachs, the first major brokerage to make this adjustment, lowered its recession probability from 45% to 35%. Barclays has dismissed recession risks altogether, while J.P. Morgan now estimates the likelihood of a recession to be 40%.

data.title: Wall St set for lower open as Trump says China violated tariff terms
data.description:
Key points:
  • Futures down: Dow 0.26%, S&P 500 0.33%, Nasdaq 0.24%
  • Regeneron slides on mixed trial data on smoker's lung drug
  • Ulta Beauty gains after raising annual profit forecast

Wall Street's main indexes were set for a lower open on Friday as trade worries deepened after U.S. President Donald Trump accused China of violating a tariff agreement, while investors assessed the latest inflation data.

"China, perhaps not surprisingly to some, HAS TOTALLY VIOLATED ITS AGREEMENT WITH US. So much for being Mr. NICE GUY!," Trump said in a post on his Truth Social platform.

"We went from a place where it felt as though investors had a pretty good handle on the direction of trade and tariffs and now that picture has become very confusing," said Art Hogan, chief market strategist at B Riley Wealth.

Futures tied to the main indexes cut some losses after data showed U.S. consumer spending increased marginally in April, with the year-on-year reading increasing to 2.1% after advancing 2.3% in March. The Fed tracks the PCE price measures for its 2% inflation target.

Traders continued to bet that the U.S. central bank will cut its target for short-term borrowing costs in September

At 08:48 a.m. ET, Dow E-minis (YMcv1) were down 110 points, or 0.26%, S&P 500 E-minis were down 19.25 points, or 0.33% and Nasdaq 100 E-minis were down 52.25 points, or 0.24%.

Most megacap and growth stocks inched lower in premarket trading, with Nvidia off 0.5% after gaining in the last session on reporting robust quarterly revenue growth. Tesla lagged with a 1% fall.

Despite the losses, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq are on pace for their best monthly showing since November 2023, while the Dow is also set for a near 4% monthly advance.

It has been a volatile month for stocks as Trump's on-and-off trade moves kept investors on edge, though his softening stance on tariffs has helped the S&P 500 rebound from its April lows. The benchmark is now about 4% lower than its all-time high hit in February.

U.S. equities had initially rallied in the previous session, after the Court of International Trade ruled late on Wednesday to effectively block most levies imposed since January, without addressing some industry-specific tariffs.

However, a federal appeals court on Thursday temporarily reinstated most of the tariffs and ordered the plaintiffs in the cases to respond by June 5 and the administration by June 9.

Hopes of more deals between the U.S. and major trading partners, along with upbeat earnings and tame inflation data, are behind most of the gains in equities this month.

Among other moving stocks, chipmaker Marvell Technology shed 3.9% despite forecasting second-quarter revenue above estimates.

Ulta Beauty gained 10% after the cosmetics retailer raised its annual profit forecast after beating quarterly results.

Shares of drugmaker Regeneron dropped 12.1% after its experimental drug for patients with a type of lung condition commonly called "smoker's lung" failed a late-stage trial, although it succeeded in another.

Lifetime Value Company Recognized Nationally and Regionally as a 2025 Best and Brightest Company to Work For(R)
data.description:
Honored by the National Association for Business Resources, Lifetime Value Company Earns Prestigious Recognition for Workplace Excellence at Both the National and New York Regional Levels

NEW YORK CITY, NEW YORK / ACCESS Newswire / May 30, 2025 / The National Association for Business Resources (NABR) has officially announced its 2025 Best and Brightest Companies to Work For®, and Lifetime Value Company is proud to be recognized, earning distinction both nationally and in the New York region.

Best and Brightest Companies to Work For® in the Nation

This prestigious recognition is awarded to organizations that demonstrate exceptional business acumen and innovative human resource practices. Given the competitive nature of this program, with a 10-to-1 ratio, these winning companies truly excel in their employee engagement strategies.

"We are honored to receive both national and regional recognition as a 2025 Best and Brightest Company to Work For," Josh Levy, CEO and co-founder of The Lifetime Value Company, said. "One of our core values is Be All In, and these awards truly speak to the passion everyone at LTVCo. has to produce their best work and maintain a thriving culture while doing it."

The winning companies were evaluated by an independent research firm based on these metrics: Compensation, Benefits, and Employee Solutions; Creative Wellness and Wellbeing Solutions; Employee Enrichment, Engagement, and Retention; Employee Education and Development; Recruitment and Selection; Employee Achievement and Recognition; Communication and Shared Vision; Civility and Inclusion; Work-Life Balance; Community Initiatives and Corporate Responsibility; and Leadership, Strategy, and Company Performance.

With over 25 years of experience conducting the Best and Brightest competitions, the NABR has identified numerous best HR practices and provided benchmarking for companies that are leaders in employment standards.

Jennifer Kluge, President and CEO of NABR and the Best and Brightest Programs, emphasized that "these companies have excelled by prioritizing their workplace culture. By putting their employees first, this exceptional group of top-tier leaders has created a strong foundation that sets them apart as leaders in their industries and communities."

These winning companies will be profiled in Corp! Magazine's All Winner Issue in December 2025.

About the National Association for Business Resources' Best and Brightest Programs

NABR offers a variety of recognition programs that celebrate excellence in different aspects of business, such as workplace culture, wellness initiatives, and leadership. These programs include the Best and Brightest Companies to Work For, Best and Brightest in Wellness, Best and Brightest CEOs, and Best and Brightest Leadership Teams that are presented in several markets that include Atlanta, Charlotte, Chicago, Dallas/Fort Worth, Denver, Detroit, Houston, Milwaukee, Nashville, New England, New York, Northern California, Pacific Northwest, Southern California, South Florida, West Michigan and nationally. Nominations are now being accepted for all programs. Visit https://nationalbiz.org/ to obtain an application. For more information, contact Jennifer Kluge at (586) 393-8813.

About The Lifetime Value Company

LTVCo. is a tech company with a culture of innovation and a passion for data. We build and ship best-in-class apps, web-based products and tools that put the power of information into the hands of our customers. Our mission is to help people discover, understand and use data in their everyday lives. Learn more at www.ltvco.com and on LinkedIn, Instagram, Facebook and X.

data.description:

30 May 2025

Nuformix plc

("Nuformix" or the "Company")

Total Voting Rights

Following the issue of new ordinary shares of 0.05p each in the Company ("Ordinary Shares"), as announced on 22 May 2025, the Company is today providing a confirmation regarding its voting rights, in line with section DTR 5.6.1 of the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules sourcebook.

As of the date of this announcement, the Company's issued ordinary share capital consists of 1,995,709,368 Ordinary Shares, each with one voting right.  The Company does not hold any Ordinary Shares in treasury.  The total voting rights figure is therefore 1,995,709,368 and this can be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine whether they are required to notify their interest in, or a change of their interest in, the Company under the Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules of the Financial Conduct Authority.

MKANGO RELEASES Q1 2025 RESULTS

London / Vancouver: 30 May 2025 - Mkango Resources Ltd (AIM / TSX-V:MKA) (the "Company" or "Mkango"), is pleased to announce that it has released the Financial Statements and Management's Discussion and Analysis for the 3-month period ending 31 March 2025. The reports are available under the Company's profile on SEDARplus (www.sedarplus.com) and on the Company's website (https://mkango.ca/investors/financials/).

To view the Financial Statements, please click here: http://www.rns-pdf.londonstockexchange.com/rns/8504K_1-2025-5-30.pdf

To view the Management Discussion and Analysis, please click here: http://www.rns-pdf.londonstockexchange.com/rns/8504K_2-2025-5-30.pdf

About Mkango Resources Ltd.

Mkango is listed on AIM and the TSX-V. Mkango's corporate strategy is to become a market leader in the production of recycled rare earth magnets, alloys and oxides, through its interest in Maginito Limited ("Maginito"), which is owned 79.4 per cent by Mkango and 20.6 per cent by CoTec, and to develop new sustainable sources of neodymium, praseodymium, dysprosium and terbium to supply accelerating demand from electric vehicles, wind turbines and other clean energy technologies.

Maginito holds a 100 per cent interest in HyProMag and a 90 per cent direct and indirect interest (assuming conversion of Maginito's convertible loan) in HyProMag GmbH, focused on short loop rare earth magnet recycling in the UK and Germany, respectively, and a 100 per cent interest in Mkango Rare Earths UK Ltd ("Mkango UK"), focused on long loop rare earth magnet recycling in the UK via a chemical route.

Maginito and CoTec are also rolling out HyProMag's recycling technology into the United States via the 50/50 owned HyProMag USA LLC joint venture company.

Mkango also owns the advanced stage Songwe Hill rare earths project and an extensive rare earths, uranium, tantalum, niobium, rutile, nickel and cobalt exploration portfolio in Malawi, and the Pulawy rare earths separation project in Poland.

Songwe Hill is one of the few rare earths projects to have progressed to the Definitive Feasibility Stage, with an expected life of mine of 18 years, producing a 55% mixed rare earth carbonate, yielding 1,953 tons per annum of NdPr and 56 tons per annum of DyTb.

Mkango's proposed Pulawy separation facility site, located in a Special Economic Zone in Poland, stands adjacent to the EU's second largest manufacturer of nitrogen fertilisers, and features established infrastructure, access to reagents and utilities on site.

Mkango has signed a letter of Intent with Crown PropTech Acquisitions to list Mkango's Songwe Hill and Pulawy Rare Earths Projects on NASDAQ via a SPAC Merger.

For more information, please visit www.mkango.ca

data.title: Napco (NSSC) Under Scrutiny: Investor Suit and Sales Slump Shake Investor Confidence - Hagens Berman
data.description:
SAN FRANCISCO, CA / ACCESS Newswire / May 30, 2025 / Shares in Napco Security Technologies, Inc. are trading down nearly 21% year-to-date, as the security technology company continues to face heightened scrutiny as it navigates both legal and operational challenges. Most recently, on May 5, 2025, Napco released its third-quarter financial results for fiscal year 2025, revealing mixed performance metrics amid an ongoing securities class action lawsuit that centers on its distribution practices and sales forecasting.

Hagens Berman is investigating the alleged claims and urges Napco investors who suffered substantial losses to submit your losses now.

Class Period: Feb. 5, 2024 - Feb. 3, 2025

Lead Plaintiff Deadline: June 24, 2025

Visit: www.hbsslaw.com/investor-fraud/nssc

Contact the Firm Now: NSSC@hbsslaw.com

844-916-0895

Third Quarter Financial Overview

Napco's third-quarter results showed a 10.8% year-over-year drop in net sales, totaling $43.96 million. While the company managed to surpass earnings-per-share expectations-reporting $0.36 per share versus a $0.29 forecast-overall revenue missed Wall Street estimates, and net income declined by over 23% compared to the previous year. The company attributed the sales shortfall primarily to inventory reductions by key distributors, a factor that has become central to the ongoing lawsuit.

Background of the Securities Class Action

The class action, filed as Patel v. Napco Security Technologies, Inc., et al., alleges that Napco's leadership misled investors about its ability to accurately predict hardware demand and sustain ambitious margin targets for fiscal 2026. The complaint claims that despite public assurances of strong hardware division growth and effective forecasting, Napco failed to disclose vulnerabilities in its distribution network and the risks associated with relying on a limited number of major distributors.

The situation escalated after the company's February 3, 2025, earnings report, which revealed a 25% decrease in equipment sales and prompted Napco to withdraw its 45% EBITDA margin target for fiscal 2026. These disclosures led to a dramatic 26% decline in Napco's share price in a single trading session, erasing nearly $10 per share in value.

Allegations and Share Price Impact

Plaintiffs allege that Napco's optimistic statements about its sales pipeline and margin outlook were misleading, given the company's inability to accurately forecast demand and the impact of distributor inventory adjustments. The lawsuit contends that these omissions artificially inflated Napco's stock price during the class period, causing substantial losses when the true financial picture emerged.

Specifically, on February 3rd, Napco released disappointing second-quarter fiscal 2025 results, revealing a substantial 25% drop in equipment sales compared to the prior year's second quarter, along with significant declines in gross margin and gross profit for equipment revenue. The company attributed this disappointing performance to "reduced sales to two of our larger distributors," with one distributor explicitly citing efforts to reduce its inventory levels.

These disclosures caused Napco's shares to plummet 26% over a single trading day.

Hagens Berman's Investigation

Prominent shareholder rights firm Hagens Berman is actively investigating the allegations against Napco, focusing on whether the company misled investors regarding its sales forecasting and distribution practices.

"Investors deserve transparency, especially when a company's future growth is tied so closely to its ability to manage demand and distribution. When those fundamentals are called into question, as we believe they are here, it's our job to ensure shareholders get clear answers and accountability," said Reed Kathrein, the Hagens Berman partner overseeing the investigation.

If you invested in Napco and have substantial losses, or have knowledge that may assist the firm's investigation, submit your losses now »

Whistleblowers: Persons with non-public information regarding Napco should consider their options to help in the investigation or take advantage of the SEC Whistleblower program. Under the new program, whistleblowers who provide original information may receive rewards totaling up to 30 percent of any successful recovery made by the SEC. For more information, call Reed Kathrein at 844-916-0895 or email NSSC@hbsslaw.com.

# # #

About Hagens Berman

Hagens Berman is a global plaintiffs' rights complex litigation firm focusing on corporate accountability. The firm is home to a robust practice and represents investors as well as whistleblowers, workers, consumers and others in cases achieving real results for those harmed by corporate negligence and other wrongdoings. Hagens Berman's team has secured more than $2.9 billion in this area of law. More about the firm and its successes can be found at hbsslaw.com. Follow the firm for updates and news at @ClassActionLaw.

data.title: Marvell Technology Price Target Maintained With a $60.00/Share by Cantor Fitzgerald
Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0920 ET - Canadian GDP growth in 1Q may have surprised to the upside at 2.2% annualized, but a contraction in domestic demand has Capital Economics sticking with its view the Bank of Canada will again cut interest rates next week. The pace was stronger than the flash estimate for a 1.5% rise, though economist Stephen Brown says that likely in part reflects the downward revision to 4Q 2024 growth to 2.1% from 2.6%. Either way, he says the details are concerning. Most of the growth came from net trade, with a 6.7% annualized rise in exports amid tariff front-running in the U.S. Canadian consumption growth slowed to 1.2% annualized, gross fixed capital formation sank 3.1% and government spending surprisingly contracted a modest 0.3%. Capital expects 2Q GDP growth will slow toward 0.5% at best. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0901 ET - Dollar indexes are flat relative to yesterday's close as trade tensions gain momentum while a key U.S. inflation gauge inches closer to the Fed's target. Trump lashes out against China's trade practices, in a social media post. Meanwhile, April PCE inflation cools to 2.1% from 2.3%. The Fed targets 2%. However, markets keep pricing a hold on interest rates through September and a total of two cuts this year. Next week's labor report is likely to be scrutinized for signs of policy impact on employment. The dollar weakens 0.3% against the yen, while strengthening 0.2% to the euro.(paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0858 ET - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0855 ET - Canada's GDP in 1Q rose at a faster-than-expected 2.2% annualized pace, or the fifth straight quarter of 2%-plus expansion. Yet, a key gauge turned negative, which the Bank of Canada will have to consider when it issues its next rate-policy decision and reflects broader weakness as tariff threats cast a pall over the economy. Final domestic demand declined 0.1%, or the first drop since late 2023. Business investment fell 3.1% in 1Q and government outlays dropped 0.3%. Those were offset by a 1.2% gain in consumer spending, although that marked a sharp slowdown from the previous quarter's 4.9% gain. Exports and inventories drove the 1Q gain, which Statistics Canada attributed to companies looking to sell and acquire goods before tariffs kicked in. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0855 ET - The euro trims its losses against the dollar after German inflation data came in slightly higher than expected. Annual inflation remained at 2.1% in May, while analysts in a WSJ survey expected it to fall to 2.0%. Meanwhile, core inflation remains significantly higher at 2.8% and there has been little progress in this area over the past 12 months, Commerzbank's Ralph Solveen says in a note. "Nevertheless, the European Central Bank is likely to cut interest rates further on Thursday." The euro falls 0.2% to $1.1347, compared with $1.1338 before the data. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0843 ET - PCE inflation is slightly lower than expected, sparking a bonds selloff that sends Treasury yields higher. Annual inflation, by the Fed's preferred gauge, ticks lower to 2.1% in April from 2.3% in March. Economists surveyed by WSJ expected 2.2%. The core reading is 2.5%, down from 2.6%. Yields had been recovering from early lows after Trump expressed frustration, in social media, with China's trade practices. The inflation report accelerated the rise, keeping yields on track for a monthly gain. The 10-year is at 4.434% and the two-year at 3.939%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0841 ET - The euro faces a further pullback against the dollar in the near-term, TD Securities strategists say in a note. Interest-rate differentials are moving in favor of the dollar, increasing the costs of protection against losses in anticipation of the currency falling, they say. Economic data have also shifted in favor of the dollar recently and there remains "very little evidence of the asset allocation reset away from the U.S." These factors increase the risks of the euro falling in the weeks ahead, especially if the market reconnects with rate differentials, they say. TD expects the euro to fall to $1.11 in the second quarter from $1.1347 currently. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 ET - The Bank of England could keep interest rates at 4.25% for the remaining part of 2025 due to inflationary pressures, Berenberg economist Andrew Wishart says in a note. The increase in companies' national insurance contributions starting in April and rise in pay growth have added to businesses' costs and are likely to translate into higher inflation, Wishart says. Markets price in a 25-basis-point BOE interest rate cut in November, and a possible second interest rate cut in December, LSEG data shows. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0836 ET - The first detailed look under the hood of Canada's economy since tariffs came into effect in early March at first blush suggests resilience, though there are cracks. GDP growth accelerated slightly to 2.2% annualized in 1Q, from downwardly revised growth of 2.1% the quarter before. That was stronger than the 1.7% expansion economists expected and 1.8% growth anticipated by the Bank of Canada. Growth was driven by exports, particularly autos and industrial machinery, which jumped in the early months of the year as U.S. importers looked to build inventories before tariffs kicked in. Offsetting that, imports were also up strongly, household spending cooled and home resale activity slumped. Business investment in non-residential structures was also down. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0756 ET - Banks raised their supply of euro-denominated senior unsecured bonds in May to nearly 22 billion euros, ING's Marine Leleux says in a note. "This brings 2025 year-to-date supply to 116 billion euro, split between just over 41 billion euro in senior preferred bonds and 74.5 billion euro in senior bail-in instruments," she says. Heavy issuance from U.S., U.K. and French banks led to elevated supply of senior bail-in bonds in May, Leleux says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0746 ET - The key risk to U.S. Treasurys is that higher coupons on newly issued debt are going to be needed to attract additional buying as deficits get bigger, AXA IM Investment Institute Chair Chris Iggo says in a note. "This pushes up market yields and pushes down prices on existing bonds, leading to negative price returns in bond portfolios," he says. For foreign investors in U.S. bonds, there is also a fear that the real value of their holdings could be eroded by higher U.S. inflation and an even weaker dollar, says Iggo, who is also CIO of AXA IM Core. Despite the unorthodox streak running through Washington, there has been no suggestion that they are going to monetize away the debt and inflation problem of fiscal sustainability, Iggo adds. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0726 ET - Consumer spending has been off to a solid start so far this year, rising by 0.5% in February and by 0.7% in March after a roughly flat reading in January. That could change as higher tariffs filter through the economy, analysts at Oxford Economics write. An appeals court Thursday left some tariffs in place as litigation proceeds. "We don't think this shock will be enough to push the economy into a recession, but the degree to which consumers increase their precautionary savings may end up being the deciding factor as to whether a recession materializes," Oxford writes. Across Wall Street the consensus forecast sees a 0.2% April increase in consumer spending; the Commerce Department data are due at 8:30am ET. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.description:
American Rebel Holdings Inc. (AREB) filed a Form 8K - Entry Into a Definitive Agreement - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on May 30, 2025.

OID Notes

On May 27, 2025, the Company entered into five two year promissory notes with five accredited investors (the "Lenders") in the gross principal amount of $450,000 (the "Notes"). An original issue discount of $67,500 and guaranteed interest of $67,500 was applied on the issuance date, resulting in net loan proceeds to the Company of $315,000. The Notes are required to be paid in one lump sum payment of $450,000 on or before May 27, 2027.

Proceeds from the Notes are to be utilized for general working capital purposes, including a potential forbearance of the Bank of America lawsuit.

Upon the occurrence and during the continuation of any Event of Default, the Notes shall become immediately due and payable and the Company will be obligated to pay to the Lenders, in full satisfaction of its obligations, an amount equal to 130% times the sum of (w) the then outstanding principal amount of the Notes plus (x) accrued and unpaid interest on the unpaid principal amount of the Notes to the date of payment plus (y) any amounts owed to the Lenders pursuant to the conversion rights referenced below.

At any time after one hundred eighty days of the issuance date of the Notes, upon five (5) business days' written notice to Lenders, the Company has the option of prepaying the outstanding principal amount of the Notes, in whole or in part, by paying to the Lenders a sum of money equal to one hundred twenty-five percent (125%) of the principal amount to be redeemed, together with any and all other sums due, accrued or payable to the Lenders arising under the Notes. During the notice period, Lenders shall have the option of converting the Notes, in whole or in part, pursuant to the terms set forth below.

At any time after one hundred eighty days of the issuance date of the Notes, the Lenders may convert the outstanding unpaid principal amount of the Notes into restricted shares of Series D Convertible Preferred Stock of the Company at $7.50 per share (each share of Series D Convertible Preferred Stock in convertible into five shares of common stock). Each Lender agreed to limit the amount of stock received to less than 4.99% of the total outstanding common stock into which the Series D Convertible Preferred Stock is convertible into. There are no warrants or other derivatives attached to these Notes. The Company granted the Lenders piggy-back registration rights on the shares of common stock issuable upon conversion of the Series D Convertible Preferred Stock. The Company agreed to reserve a number of shares of Series D Convertible Preferred Stock, and common stock issuable upon conversion thereof, equal to three times the number of shares of Series D Convertible Preferred Stock (153,000 shares of Series D Convertible Preferred Stock in total), and common stock issuable upon conversion thereof (765,000 shares of common stock in total), which may be issuable upon conversion of the Notes at all times.

The foregoing description of the Notes and of all of the parties' rights and obligations under the Notes are qualified in their entirety by reference to the Form of OID Note, a copy of which is filed as Exhibit 10.1 to this Current Report on Form 8-K, and of which is incorporated herein by reference.

data.title: Summit Says Ivonescimab Significantly Improves Progression-Free Survival; Overall Survival Trend Not Statistically Significant -- Shares Drop
data.description:
Summit Therapeutics said Friday that its late-stage study of ivonescimab plus chemotherapy met the progression-free survival primary endpoint in patients with EGFR-mutated non-small cell lung cancer.

The treatment reduced the risk of disease progression or death by 48% compared to chemotherapy alone, with consistent results seen across Asian and non-Asian subgroups, Summit said.

A positive trend was observed in overall survival, though the data did not meet statistical significance, the company said.

Adverse events in the ivonescimab arm were in line with expectations and manageable, Summit said, with no new safety issues identified.

The company said it plans to file a Biologics License Application in the US, pending further evaluation and discussions with the US Food and Drug Administration.

Shares of the company were down 15% in recent Friday premarket activity.

data.title: Gold Trading Lower on a Higher Dollar as U.S. Inflation Eased in April
data.description:
Gold traded lower early on Friday as the dollar rose after data showed a key measure of U.S. inflation fell more than expected last month.

Gold for August delivery was last seen down US$19.00 to US$3.324.90 per ounce.

The U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis reported the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Index, the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation measure, rose by an annualized 2.1% in April, down from a 2.3% pace in March and under the FactSet consensus estimate for a 2.2% rise. Core PCE, excluding volatile items, rose 2.5%, down from 2.7% a month earlier while meeting the consensus estimate.

The report is not expected to change the outlook for U.S. interest rates, with the Federal Reserve's policy committee widely expected to hold rates steady at the June 18 end of its next two-day meeting. The CME Fedwatch Tool sees a 98% probability the central bank will keep rates steady, with no cut expected until September.

The dollar rose following the PCE data, with the ICE dollar index last seen up 0.14 points to 99.42. Treasury yields were little changed, with the yield on the U.S. two-year note down 0.6 basis points to 3.941%, while the 10-year note was paying 4.416%, down 0.8 points.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0915 ET - Oil and gas will be needed as energy sources and for the manufacturing of essential products for decades to come, Repsol's Chairman Antonio Brufau said Friday. Europe lacks a strategic plan for the transition away from refined fossil fuels, he said. "More than 95% of passenger and freight transport in Europe is powered by liquid fuels produced in refineries," he added. Brufau called for a reduced tax regime for renewable fuels, saying it would facilitate investment and boost consumption. Shares trade up 0.9% at 11.9 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0914 ET - Julius Baer needs to show that growth will be restored and that its flow rate trajectory will increase to lift its share price, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note ahead of the Swiss private bank's investor day on Tuesday. "We expect it to be a relatively tame affair as the group displays its new disciplined approach to risk, culture, and profitability," analysts write, noting that investor confidence is low due to a series of credit missteps. The move to streamline operations under the leadership of the new CEO since the start of the year has been a helpful first step, the analysts say. However, demonstrating strong sustainable growth is arguably a more powerful driver, they add. Shares have fallen 7.8% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0913 ET - Gap reported solid 1Q results but tariffs could weigh on operating income growth, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. The apparel company behind Old Navy, Banana Republic and its namesake brand exceeded formal consensus estimates for 1Q, but its 2% sales growth and 51 cent EPS may have fallen short of buy-side expectations, they add. The analysts expect Gap and Old Navy to continue to perform well and gain market share. But given the magnitude of the potential tariff impact — which the company estimates may total up to $300 million in added costs this year, though it has plans to offset more than half of that amount — they stick with their hold rating and lower their price target to $26 from $29. Shares tumble 15% to $23.61 in premarket trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0908 ET - Repsol's chairman is calling on Europe to use pragmatism when developing energy transition policies in order to avoid harming competitiveness. Speaking at the Spanish oil company's annual general meeting, Antonio Brufau says that Europe needs to create a true energy union, and that it currently relies too much on Russia and China for energy. It makes no sense for Europe to pursue decarbonization strategies in isolation from others, especially when its carbon-dioxide emissions are significantly lower than China and the U.S., he says. Repsol will continue to invest oil and natural gas, as well as new renewable fuels, he adds. Shares trade up 0.7% at 11.87 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0859 ET - Zscaler's latest quarterly report, in which it topped analyst estimates and raised guidance, represents another breakout moment for the cybersecurity stalwart amid a customer shift toward cloud-based security solutions, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. Zscaler is seeing strong demand for its zero trust security platform and its AI security solutions, which will be well received by Wall Street in a choppy spending environment, the analysts say. The company is scoring new clients and reeling in more customers that provide $1 million or more in annual recurring revenue, they say, maintaining an outperform rating on the stock and moving their price target up to $300 from $240. Shares rise 4.9% to $263.50. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0858 ET - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0818 ET - Marvell Technology's lack of data center upside in 1Q and its 2Q guidance leaves more questions about the current ramp of the artificial intelligence business, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The chip maker logged adjusted EPS of 62 cents and revenue of $1.9 billion in 1Q, compared to expectations of 61 cents a share in adjusted EPS and revenue of $1.88 billion. Marvell guided for adjusted EPS between 62 cents and 72 cents and sales of $2 billion, while the Street expected 66 cents a share in adjusted EPS and sales of $1.98 billion. "In short, without data center and AI upside in the year, the stock is a 'show-me story' and may have trouble breaking out," the analysts say. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0815 ET - Equity markets held steady this week amid the latest trade developments and, with U.S. President Trump's tariffs in limbo, fatigue is evident, Barclays equity strategists say in a research note. While Trump's decision to push back a deadline on EU tariffs after a call with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen offered relief to markets, there are signs that calm is returning, the strategists say. "It also shows that investors are reacting more calmly to tariff headlines now, viewing them increasingly as negotiation tactics," they add. The Stoxx Europe 600 is up 0.2% and gained 0.7% since the start of the week. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0812 ET - JPMorgan starts coverage of Xylem with an overweight rating, calling the pure-play water-technology company a "winner in the world of water." In a note, JPMorgan says Xylem is an attractive core long-term holding for industrial and sustainability focused investors seeking exposure to resilient and diversified water end markets, many of which carry multi-decade secular tailwinds. JPMorgan sets a year-end price target of $148 on Xylem shares and says currently trading at a discount to historical averages, we view current trading levels represent an attractive entry point. Xylem up 0.5% premarket to $126.30. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

0806 ET - Marvell Technology's latest results may have fallen short of some investors' lofty expectations but the company still has plenty of tailwinds, Benchmark's Cody Acree says in a research note. The chip maker's modest upside results and guidance compared to the standout performance by Nvidia is weighing on its stock. But Acree notes that on a comparative basis, the two companies are seeing similar trends with broad based demand strength in AI driving data center growth in both compute and networking. While Acree understands investor's caution, he's encouraged by a few factors including Marvell's market leading interconnect portfolio and the broad cyclical recovery it's seeing in both its carrier infrastructure and enterprise networking business, which he expects to be a positive catalyst for sentiment and its shares. Shares decline 3.4% to $61.59 in premarket trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0721 ET - The dollar is gradually losing its safe-haven status and this presents an opportunity for the eurozone, ING's chief economist Marieke Blom says in a note. "The EU now has a unique chance to capitalize on investor doubts and promote the euro as a reserve currency, a move that would yield significant economic benefits." Greater demand for euros means cheaper borrowing costs for the bloc's governments, corporations and homeowners, she says. Positioning in the euro as an alternative haven also brings stability during economic downturns. The EU needs to move quickly and start designing acceptable frameworks for issuing eurozone bonds if it wishes to take advantage of U.S. weakness. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0708 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.1% at $9,558.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.2% at $2,444.50 a ton. A stronger U.S. dollar is keeping downward pressure on metal prices, making it more expensive for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. Tariff uncertainty is also hitting metals demand, as a federal appeals court allowed U.S. President Trump's levies to stay in effect while the administration appeals a court decision that found them illegal. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has also said that trade talks with China are "a bit stalled" and may need direct contact between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, raising further concerns for metal demand. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0905 ET - Oil futures are lower with the market widely expecting OPEC+ to raise its output targets by another 411,000 barrels in July. The bearish factors that have driven the selloff this year, including tariffs and OPEC+ supply, may be mostly priced in by now, analysts at Citi say in a note. Oil has found support in "geopolitical risks vis-à-vis Iran and Russia-Ukraine, still-to-be-replenished low inventories, and firm refined product demand and cracks." The lack of significant increase in OPEC+ exports despite the accelerated unwinding of cuts has bolstered the view that the market might not turn as loose as previously expected, while Middle Eastern countries are building crude inventory for increased fuel use during the summer cooling season, they say. WTI is down 1.1% at $60.29, and Brent for August delivery is down 1.2% at $62.59 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0708 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.1% at $9,558.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.2% at $2,444.50 a ton. A stronger U.S. dollar is keeping downward pressure on metal prices, making it more expensive for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. Tariff uncertainty is also hitting metals demand, as a federal appeals court allowed U.S. President Trump's levies to stay in effect while the administration appeals a court decision that found them illegal. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has also said that trade talks with China are "a bit stalled" and may need direct contact between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, raising further concerns for metal demand. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0707 ET - Gold futures fall, but hold above the lows seen in the prior session. Futures are down 0.75% at $3,318.80 a troy ounce. The precious metal fell as low as $3,269.90/oz in volatile trading on Thursday after a U.S. court declared most tariffs imposed by President Trump were illegal, but recovered quickly on uncertainty around whether levies would be suspended immediately. An appeals court ruled that the ruling will be paused for now and tariffs would remain in effect. The trade conflict is far from over as the ruling only concerns "reciprocal" tariffs rather than sector ones, which means the economic outlook remains gloomy and interest rate cut expectations will likely remain strong, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. This should benefit gold overall given the associated safe-haven demand, Commerzbank says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0621 ET - Palm oil fell during the Asian session. Prices were likely pressured by the weaker overnight performance in rival oils and possible profit-taking after the recent sessions of gains, analysts at Kenanga Futures write in a note. A stronger ringgit also likely made exports less attractive, they add. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for August delivery fell MYR54 to MYR3,878 a ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0357 ET - Crude oil ekes out some gains on rising gasoline demand hopes, despite concerns of increased OPEC+ supply. Brent crude and WTI are both up 0.3% at $63.52 and $61.14 a barrel, respectively. A huge spike in gasoline demand and dip in stocks ahead of the U.S. driving season has buoyed oil, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. That said, OPEC+ is expected to announce production hikes for July over the weekend. The exact size of the cartel's hike will be declared at the meeting but is expected to be larger than currently scheduled, ANZ writes. Sentiment toward oil hasn't been helped by a broader selloff across markets after data showed the U.S. economy shrank at the start of the year, ANZ adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0347 ET - Gold futures slide on a stronger U.S. dollar. Futures are down 0.8% at $3,317.0 a troy ounce. The precious metal is on track for an overall weekly loss, though it remains up more than 22% in the year to date. Gold appears pressured by a stronger dollar, an alternative safe-haven asset in the face of market volatility. Market attention now turns to the release of Personal Consumption Expenditure data due later Friday. The inflation report--the preferred metric of the Federal Reserve--is expected to show prices moderated last month, opening the door for the Fed to ease monetary policy. Lower interest rates typically boost non-interest bearing bullion's appeal. Economists surveyed by FactSet expect consumer prices to rise 0.15% in April, lowering the annual rate to 2.2% from 2.3% in March. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0314 ET - Chocoladefabriken Lindt & Spruengli stood out during past six months, when record cocoa prices and a challenging consumer backdrop posed the ultimate test for chocolate brands, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. A strong share performance by the Swiss chocolatier in the year to date left many investors wondering about the prospects for the stock, Morgan Stanley says. However, the past six months proved the robustness of Lindt's brands and business model and consensus expectations on Lindt's earnings face limited risks, the analysts say. "Its ability to defend its FY25 guidance is also attractive in the current backdrop where corporate earning pressures have increased, and cocoa market risks are also rolling over," the analysts say. Shares rise 1.4% and are up 29% since the start of 2025. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)Nestle's shares closed 1.35% lower at 87.51 francs on Wednesday, with markets in Switzerland closed Thursday for a holiday. "Nestle's Organic Growth Likely to Be Offset by Swiss Franc Strength — Market Talk," at 0603 GMT, misstated the day.

0203 ET - Nestle is facing rising currency headwinds, with recent strength in the Swiss franc relative to the dollar likely to offset the company's organic sales growth, Berenberg analysts say in a research note. "Since the start of 2025, the Swiss franc has strengthened by 10% versus the U.S. dollar, and our model suggests the currency translation effect to Nestle's group sales in 2025 will be [minus] 4.2%, more than offsetting organic sales growth of 3.4%," the analysts say. Currency headwinds are set to hurt the Swiss food company's profitability as well, they add. Berenberg cuts its target price on Nestle to 87 francs from 90 francs. Shares closed 1.35% lower at 87.51 francs on Wednesday with markets closed Thursday for a holiday, and are up nearly 17% since the start of 2025. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This market talk item was corrected at 0633 GMT to reflect Nestle's shares closed 1.35% lower at 87.51 francs on Wednesday. The original version incorrectly misstated the day as Thursday in the last sentence of the item.

2255 ET - Palm oil falls in early Asian trade, tracking overnight losses in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Market sentiment may remain uncertain and volatile, it says, though technical indicators suggest crude-palm-oil futures may be supported. AmInvestment Bank sees support at 3,850 ringgit a ton and resistance at 3,998 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for August delivery is down 61 ringgit to 3,871 ringgit a ton.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2244 ET - Iron ore falls in early Asian trade. Steel demand is in its off-season period, with domestic demand falling, given weather conditions, while the rush for exports are weakening, with the number of shipments from China to the U.S. falling marginally, analysts at Nanhua Futures say, noting the import demand from the U.S. is lower than expected. Fundamentals for iron ore remain good but the market is pessimistic about the future demand, they add. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is down 0.5% at CNY701.50/ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

2145 ET - Copper is flat in early trading. The base metal is likely supported by demand in China, ANZ Research analysts say in commentary. Another summer of extreme heat could put China's electricity network under pressure and boost demand for copper, they note. Meanwhile, the country's ongoing electrification is pushing electricity demand up faster than economic activity, ANZ adds. The three-month LME copper contract is flat at $9,561.00 a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)
